title: Chelsea buns
subtitle: Yeasted spiral buns with cinnamon butter and dried fruit
excerpt: An enriched yeasted dough rolled with a cinnamon and brown sugar butter, scattered with sultanas and currants, rolled up, sliced, and baked close together so the buns pull apart. A sticky golden glaze finishes them. One of the classic British bun styles, associated with the Chelsea Bun House from the 18th century.
sourceNotes: The Chelsea Bun House operated in Pimlico from the early 18th century and gave the bun its name. A recipe for Chelsea buns appears in Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg, as an enriched milk dough rolled with butter and sugar and baked close together. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, records a similar enriched bun with currants and peel. The rolled spiral format and the sticky glaze are consistent features across the historical record.

content.text: The Chelsea bun is built from a single enriched dough: the method of

produces the spiral pattern and distributes the filling evenly through every bite. Baking the cut buns close together in a square tin means they rise into each other and form soft sides; pulling them apart at the table is part of the experience. The glaze goes on hot, so it soaks into the top.

Combine flour, yeast, sugar, and salt. Add warm milk, melted butter, and egg; mix to a soft dough. Knead for 8 minutes until smooth and elastic. Cover and rise for 60 minutes until doubled. Mix the softened butter for the filling with the brown sugar and cinnamon to a paste.

Knock back the dough and roll out on a lightly floured surface to a 30 × 40 cm rectangle, the long edge facing you. Spread the cinnamon butter evenly over the dough, leaving a 1 cm clear strip along the far long edge. Scatter the sultanas, currants, and peel over the butter. Roll the dough firmly toward the clear strip, using the clear edge to seal. Cut the log into 9 equal slices (about 4 cm each). Arrange cut-side up in a greased 23 cm square tin, 3 × 3. Cover and prove for 30 minutes until puffed and nearly touching.

Bake at 190°C conventional (170°C fan) for 22 to 25 minutes until the tops are deep gold. While the buns bake, warm the golden syrup and water together in a small pan until combined. As soon as the buns come out of the oven, brush the glaze generously over the tops. Leave to cool in the tin for 10 minutes before lifting out.
